plain_0: SaaS M&A has an information asymmetry problem. The seller knows the data, computes the metrics,
plain_1: and presents the summary. The buyer trusts. This manifesto is the line in the sand.
plain_2: When you buy a SaaS business, the seller hands you a CIM with a churn number. That number
plain_3: is computed using methodology choices the seller made. Every choice — whether to include
plain_4: downgrades, how to handle involuntary cancellations, which cohort to report — is legally
plain_5: defensible. Every choice happens to make the number smaller.
text_6: 80% of SaaS acquisitions overpay because of this gap. The average real churn
plain_7: is 4.2x the reported number. On a $1M deal, that's $340K of evaporated value. The buyer never
plain_8: sees it coming because the methodology choices are invisible in a summary metric.
text_9: This isn't an improvement on the old way. It's a completely different approach.
plain_10: The old way asks "what does the seller report?" The new way asks "what does the raw data actually
plain_11: show?" Those are fundamentally different questions with fundamentally different answers.
plain_12: The long-term vision: a SaaS acquisition market where buyer-side churn analysis is the default,
plain_13: not the exception. Where no buyer trusts a summary churn number. Where sellers know that any
plain_14: methodology tricks will be caught in minutes by automated analysis. When that happens, the
plain_15: information asymmetry closes. Deals get priced honestly. Buyers stop overpaying.
plain_16: Churn Lens is the tool. The checklist is the entry point. The raw-data methodology is the principle.
plain_17: And the movement is every acquirer who reads this and demands the CSV on their next deal.
